title: Air-fryer parsnips
subtitle: Honey-glazed parsnip batons in 22 minutes
excerpt: Parsnip batons tossed in vegetable oil and honey, cooked in the air fryer at 200°C for 20 to 22 minutes. The honey caramelises in the heat and balances the natural sweetness of the parsnip with a slightly sticky, golden finish. A faster alternative to oven-roasted parsnips with comparable results.
sourceNotes: Parsnips are a British winter vegetable closely associated with the Sunday roast. They are typically honey-glazed or roasted alongside the Sunday joint. The air fryer produces a comparable result to oven-roasting in roughly half the time and without heating a large oven for a single side dish.

content.text: Cut parsnips into batons no thicker than 1.5 cm at the widest point. Parsnip is denser than potato and takes longer to cook through. Large thick pieces will be raw at the centre by the time the outside has browned and the honey has caramelised. The thinner baton shape also gives more surface area for the glaze to coat.

items.name: Parsnip
items.prepNote: peeled and cut into batons, no thicker than 1.5 cm

content.text: Toss the {{parsnip}} batons with {{vegetable-oil}}, {{honey}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{black-pepper}}, and {{thyme}} if using until evenly coated. Preheat the air fryer to 200°C for 3 minutes. Arrange in a single layer in the basket. Cook at 200°C for 20 to 22 minutes, turning once at the 10-minute mark, until golden and lightly caramelised on the surfaces and completely tender when pierced with a knife at the thickest point. The honey will have formed a sticky, golden glaze. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Keep batons to 1.5 cm. Use exactly 1 tablespoon of honey for 600g: more than this caramelises and burns before the parsnip is cooked through. Reduce to 190°C if the glaze is darkening too fast
items.cause: Batons too thick, or too much honey
items.symptom: Honey burning and turning dark brown before parsnips are cooked

items.fix: The thick end of a large parsnip can be 3 to 4 cm across. Quarter these lengthways rather than halving. Consistent baton size is the main factor in even cooking
items.cause: Batons too thick, especially if the parsnip was very large
items.symptom: Parsnips cooked on the outside but still firm in the centre

items.fix: Spray the basket with oil before adding the parsnips. Turn gently at 10 minutes: if they are stuck, give them another minute before attempting to turn
items.symptom: Parsnips sticking together and to the basket

content.text: Air-fryer honey parsnips are the fastest route to a side dish that usually requires a 40-minute oven roast. Serve alongside a Sunday roast chicken, with sausages and mash, or as a winter side dish for pork. They pair particularly well with dishes that have a mustard or apple note.
